home.eyebrow: Find it. Check in. Pass it on.
home.headline: A lighter with a history. Add yours.
home.subheadline: Find a lighter with a QR sticker. Scan it to discover everywhere it's been and everyone who held it. Leave your own note — then pass it on to the next stranger.
home.lighterAlt: A lighter lying on its side

errors.missingDash: Missing the dash — it should be a name, a dash, then a number.
errors.nameTooShort: The name before the dash is too short — it needs at least 3 letters (you typed "{{prefix}}").
errors.noNumber: Nothing after the dash — add a 2-digit number.
errors.numberRequired: The part after the dash should be a number.
errors.notFound: We couldn't find <lighterName>{{id}}</lighterName>. Double-check the sticker — it should look like <lighterName>{{example}}</lighterName>.

howItWorks.heading: Here's the deal.
howItWorks.step1Body: Spot a lighter with a QR sticker. Scan it, or type its ID on litroute.com. Every city, every hand it passed through — it's all there waiting for you.
howItWorks.step2Body: Drop a photo and a quick note. Your moment — where you were, what you saw — becomes a permanent chapter in the lighter's story.
howItWorks.step3Body: Hand it to a stranger. Leave it at a coffee shop, a hostel, a trailhead. You decide the next chapter.
howItWorks.step4Body: Subscribe and get an email the next time someone finds it — wherever in the world that turns out to be. Low-stakes stalking of a piece of metal.

cta.heading: Start a journey.
cta.body: I'm Jonas, I made this while funemployed. Want to send a lighter into the world? <aboutLink>Read the about page</aboutLink> first — you'll need to get in touch before you start printing labels.

stats.freeText: Free to use, kept alive by readers like you. <supportLink>Support the project →</supportLink>

journey.heading: Here's what you'll see when you scan one.
journey.subtext: These are made-up check-ins to show you how it works.
journey.openSlotBody: Find one, check in, and you could be here.

home.globeCaption: 20 recently active lighters — each dot is someone's story

about.header: For people who leave things behind on purpose.
about.subheader: A lighter travels from hand to hand. Each owner checks in — a photo, a location, a message. This is where you follow the journey.

why.a: Because I wanted to. I'm funemployed, and I thought: what if a lighter could tell you where it had been?

how.a: Built with Django and React. Open source — <githubLink>GitHub repo</githubLink>. PRs welcome after opening an issue.

who.a: My name is Jonas. You can <emailLink>send me an email</emailLink> or reach me via <mastodonLink>@jvacek@fosstodon.org</mastodonLink>

secure.a1: Storing as little as possible is a design goal, not an afterthought. We don't store passwords at all — login is via a magic code sent to your email, so there's nothing to leak. The EXIF data on images is stripped, and your location is whatever you type in manually.
secure.a2: Don't believe me? The code is open for everyone to see. You can even run your own LitRoute, just please call it something else.

money.q: Will you try to make money off of this?
money.a1: Honestly, I don't know. I'm not planning on it. I'd much rather keep funding this from my own pocket and donations.
money.a2: This is my little personal project that I want to work on purely for fun, and when you start making money off of something it can quickly become a source of stress, so I'd rather avoid that.
money.a3: Either way, your personal identifiable information will never be sold or given to anyone.

help.q: Is there any way I can help?
help.a1: Sure! Even if you don't code, I'm looking for people who would be happy to buy their own lighters and register them into the system. I'm not at the stage where I can make this self-service, so just reach out to me using the contact info above.
help.a2: If you do code, I'm happy to accept pull requests, just make sure to open an issue first so we can discuss it!

support.subheader: LitRoute is free and independent. Here's what keeps it running.

supportSection.body1: LitRoute is a one-person project. There are no ads, no subscriptions, and no investors — just server costs, email delivery, and storage coming out of my own pocket. That includes the lighters themselves: each one is bought and registered by hand before it can start its journey.
supportSection.body2: If you've enjoyed following a journey here, a small contribution goes a long way.

terms.title: Terms of Service
terms.body: Full terms are coming soon. The basics: be kind, don't abuse the platform, and don't use it for anything illegal. This is a small personal project run for fun.

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.body: We're working on a full policy. The short version: we store as little as possible, never sell your data, and only use your email to send you notifications you've opted into.

giveToWhom.q: Who should I give the lighter to?
giveToWhom.a1: Anyone you want. It's your lighter, you decide. I recommend giving it to someone who you think will enjoy silliness, and who can help the lighter travel as far as possible; someone who you think will come across more people with a similar spirit. Maybe someone who's been having a rough time lately?
giveToWhom.a2: Otherwise, maybe you can also try leaving it in a busy place like at a bar, a club smoking room, airport lounges, or any other place you think like-minded people might end up in. It's a bit of a risk though, because it might get thrown away by someone who doesn't know what it is, or they won't be curious enough to check the site.

multipleCheckins.q: Should I make more than one check-in?
multipleCheckins.a1: Make as many check-ins as you want, but I definitely recommend making at least one when you get the lighter. You can also keep making ones whenever you go to a significant place, like a different town, country, or a place you think is cool like a festival or the beach.
multipleCheckins.a2: The idea is that you see the lighter travel from hand to hand and from place to place. As a rule of thumb, check-in when you would expect/want other people to check-in.

anythingDo.q: Anything I should do with the lighter?
anythingDo.a1: If you can, please top up the lighter before passing it on. That way it will travel that little bit further.
anythingDo.a2: You can also doodle on it, or decorate it in any way you want.

lightDied.q: What do I do if the lighter died?
lightDied.a: If you can, please refill it. If you can't, please pass it on to someone else.

cantSeeAll.q: Why can I not see all the lighters?
cantSeeAll.a1: It's an arbitrary choice I made, but I feel like I'd like to keep the experience special and personal to those who have actually had the lighters in their hands. Hopefully this will also encourage you to be more personal with your check-ins as you won't worry about the whole world seeing your messages.
cantSeeAll.a2: Want to write a little love-letter to your crush which they get to read when they check the lighter's history? Go ahead, no one will see it but them (and the people who they will pass the lighter to, so watch out lmao).

contributorGuide.subheader: A practical guide for contributors: how to register a new unit, label the lighter, and send it on its way.
contributorGuide.greeting: Hey — thank you for being here.
contributorGuide.greetingBody1: Honestly, LitRoute only works because people like you are willing to buy lighters, label them, and trust strangers to keep them moving. That's the whole thing. Without that, it's just a website.
contributorGuide.greetingBody2: I started this because I wanted to see how far a lighter could travel, and every time I get a notification from one of them I still find it kind of magical. I hope you do too. If you ever have feedback, ideas, or just want to share how one of your lighters is doing — please reach out. It genuinely means a lot.
contributorGuide.passingOnSubtext: The same questions recipients will have — useful to know before you hand the lighter over.
contributorGuide.footerText: Something unclear or missing? <aboutLink>Visit the about page</aboutLink> or get in touch.

createUnit.a: In the admin panel, go to Backend › Units and click Add unit. Use a first name and a number as the identifier (e.g. john-93, leila-23). At least three characters before the dash, at least two digits after. The identifier becomes part of the URL, so keep it short and readable.
createUnit.tip: Pick names with one obvious spelling — avoid ambiguous ones like Steven vs. Stephen or Jeffrey vs. Jeffery. If someone has to guess to find the URL, you've already lost them.

makeLabel.a: Write the unit's name in all caps and the main domain on the label — e.g. JOHN-93 and LITROUTE.COM. A QR code pointing to the unit URL makes it even easier for recipients to check in without having to type anything.
makeLabel.tip: Also add a short nudge so people know what to do: "Scan me, tag me, pass me on" or "Check in. Keep me moving."

attachLabel.a: Stick the label to the lighter in a way that will last — clear packing tape over the top works well. The label needs to survive being pocketed and passed around, so make sure the edges are properly sealed. Orient the text so it runs along the length of the lighter, not across it — that way someone can read the whole line without having to rotate it halfway through.
attachLabel.tip: Consider doodling or drawing on the lighter too. A lighter with a face or a little character is harder to throw away — give it a personality and people will want to keep it going.

firstCheckin.a: Visit your unit's page and make the first check-in yourself. This seeds the journey and gives the next person something to see when they scan the code. You were automatically subscribed when you created the unit, so you'll get an email every time someone checks in.

handOff.a: Give the lighter to someone and let it travel. You don't need to do anything else — just watch the notifications come in.
